attack_chain.description: Attackers launched a wide social media phishing campaign targeting IT and security department employees, using social engineering to convince them to download and execute malicious payloads hidden in technical programs and data files.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: attackers initiated a wide social media phishing campaign targeting company employees... Clever social engineering was used to convince these employees to download and execute malicious payloads concealed in a variety of technical programs and data files.

attack_chain.description: A cloud DevOps engineer executed the malicious infostealer payload on their personal, unmanaged home computer, which lacked EDR protection, giving attackers full control of the device.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: a cloud dev-ops engineer executed the malicious payload on their personal home computer which contained no sensitive company information... the unmanaged home device lacked the EDR installed on corporate devices which may have prevented execution of the malicious payload.

attack_chain.description: With full control of the compromised employee laptop, attackers extracted valid Citrix (VDP) session cookies to hijack an active privileged session into the corporate network.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: attackers to extract valid Citrix session cookies from the compromised employee laptop and successfully hijack a valid privileged session into the corporate network.

attack_chain.description: Using the hijacked Citrix session, attackers gained RDP access to a jump server that was used to manage the company's production AWS environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: This session contained direct RDP access to a jump server used to access and manage the company's production AWS environment.

attack_chain.technique_name: Unsecured Credentials: Cloud Instance Metadata API
attack_chain.description: From the compromised on-premises jump server, attackers extracted highly privileged AWS access keys, enabling direct access to the production cloud environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: From this server attackers were able to extract highly privileged AWS access keys and proceed to the main phase of the attack.

attack_chain.description: Attackers rapidly performed automated cloud reconnaissance using pre-existing scripts and techniques to identify S3 buckets, RDS databases, EC2 instances, and VPCs across the production environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The speed at which this phase of the attack was executed leaves little doubt that much of it was automated leveraging pre-existing cloud reconnaissance scripts and techniques.

attack_chain.description: Attackers began collecting and exfiltrating data from S3 storage buckets and sensitive RDS databases containing client data.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: they began exfiltrating data from S3 storage buckets and sensitive RDS databases containing client data

attack_chain.description: In parallel with exfiltration, attackers installed reverse shells on EC2 instances across multiple VPCs to establish persistent footholds within the cloud environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: installing reverse shells on EC2 instances in multiple VPCs

attack_chain.technique_name: Indicator Removal: Clear Linux or Mac System Logs
attack_chain.description: Attackers deleted local OS logs from compromised EC2 instances to prevent forensic analysis of logins and executed commands.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Deleting local OS logs from compromised EC2 instances to prevent analysis of logins and executed commands

attack_chain.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Logs
attack_chain.description: Attackers modified S3 and RDS audit policies to suppress audit logging and prevent the organization from determining which data was being accessed.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: modifying S3 and RDS audit policies to prevent the organization from knowing exactly which data was being accessed

attack_chain.description: After defenders revoked AWS credentials, attackers fell back to the compromised on-premises jump server — and then to the compromised employee laptop — to re-establish access, cycling through three persistent footholds for over 17 months.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Once their access to AWS was revoked, they simply acquired new access through the compromised on-premises jump server... all they had to do was simply go back to the compromised employee laptop, wait for a new session on the jump server, and they were back in.

initial_access_vector: Social media phishing campaign targeting IT/security employees; malicious payload executed on an unmanaged personal home computer lacking EDR
persistence_mechanism: Three-layer hybrid persistence: (1) reverse shells on EC2 instances, (2) compromised on-premises jump server with RDP, (3) compromised employee laptop with Citrix session cookie theft — enabling repeated re-entry over 17+ months
privilege_escalation: Citrix (VDP) session cookie theft used to hijack a valid privileged corporate session; subsequent extraction of highly privileged AWS access keys from the on-premises jump server
